items.notes: MSA uses the formal Islamic greeting; Lebanese commonly uses 'marḥaba' for casual hello. 'Salām' is also used in Lebanese but shortened.
Lebanese often uses the French-influenced 'bāy' (bye) or 'yalla bāy'. 'Maʕ salēme' also exists but is less casual.
Very similar in both, but Lebanese drops the case endings and shortens the vowels. The response in Lebanese is 'ṣbāḥ in-nūr'.
Lebanese contracts 'kayfa ḥāluka' into the single word 'kīfak' (m) / 'kīfik' (f). Much shorter and more fluid.
Lebanese uses 'mnīḥ' (good/fine), which has no MSA equivalent. It comes from a Syriac-Aramaic root. MSA uses 'bi-khayr' (in goodness).
One of the most distinctive differences. MSA 'mādhā' becomes Lebanese 'shū', derived from 'ayyu shayʔ' (which thing).
MSA 'ʔayna' becomes Lebanese 'wēn', a contracted form. Very common across Levantine dialects.
Lebanese adds a prefix 'ʔē-' to 'matā'. Also heard as 'ʔēmtēn' in some regions.
Similar root, but Lebanese simplifies the vowels and drops case endings. 'Kīf' is universal in Lebanese.
MSA 'limādhā' contracts to 'lēsh' in Lebanese, from 'li-ayyi shayʔ' (for what thing).
Similar root but vowel shift from 'a' to 'ī'. Lebanese 'mīn' is used across all Levantine dialects.
Completely different verbs. MSA uses 'dhahaba'; Lebanese uses 'rāḥ' (lit. 'to fly/go away'), which also means 'went'.
MSA 'yaʔtī' becomes 'yiji' in Lebanese, from the root j-y-ʔ. The hamza is dropped and vowels shift.
Different verbs entirely. MSA uses 'raʔā' (to see); Lebanese uses 'shāf' (to look/see), common across Levantine Arabic.
Same root (ʔ-k-l) but Lebanese drops the hamza and simplifies vowels. Pronunciation is noticeably different.
Same root (sh-r-b). Lebanese drops case endings and shifts the initial vowel from 'ya-' to 'yi-'.
Different verbs. MSA uses Form V 'takallama'; Lebanese uses 'ḥaka' (to narrate/talk), a simpler form.
Almost identical. Lebanese shortens the final vowel slightly: 'ʔana' vs MSA 'ʔanā'.
Lebanese shifts the hamza vowel from 'a' to 'i': 'ʔinta' instead of 'ʔanta'.
Same vowel shift as masculine: 'ʔinti' instead of 'ʔanti'. Sometimes extended to 'ʔintī'.
Lebanese adds an emphatic ending '-we' to become 'huwwe'. Very characteristic of Lebanese dialect.
Parallel to 'huwwe', the feminine becomes 'hiyye' in Lebanese, adding the '-ye' ending.
Lebanese shifts to 'niḥna'. The vowel change and final '-a' are distinctive Lebanese features.
Same root but Lebanese drops the tanwīn (nunation) ending and shifts the vowel from 'i' to 'a'.
Significantly shortened in Lebanese. The initial hamza and 'th' sound become just 't', and the whole word contracts.
Lebanese replaces 'th' (ث) with 't' throughout, a hallmark of Lebanese phonology. MSA 'thalāthah' becomes 'tlēte'.
Similar but Lebanese compresses the vowels and drops endings. The 'ʕayn' remains prominent in both.
Minor differences: Lebanese drops the tanwīn and the final vowel shifts. Pronunciation is close between the two.
Same root (sh-t-r-y) but Lebanese shifts the prefix vowel from 'ya-' to 'yi-' and shortens internal vowels.
Same root (f-t-ḥ). Lebanese drops the final case vowel and uses the 'yi-' prefix pattern.
Different verbs entirely. MSA uses 'ʔaghlaqa' (Form IV); Lebanese uses 'sakar' (to lock/close), from a different root.
Same root (ṭ-b-kh). Minimal difference -- mainly the prefix vowel shift and dropped endings typical of Lebanese.
Same root (gh-s-l). Lebanese follows the standard pattern: 'yi-' prefix and no case endings.
Same root (n-w-m). Lebanese shortens the vowel pattern and drops endings. Often heard as 'ynēm' with an 'ē' vowel.
Lebanese dramatically shortens 'ʕalā' to just 'ʕa'. One of the most recognizable Lebanese contractions.
Lebanese often replaces 'fī' with the prefix 'b-' attached directly to the next word. 'Fī' still exists but means 'there is'.
Lebanese replaces 'ʔilā' with 'la-' (prefix) or 'ʕa' depending on context. 'ʔilā' is not used in spoken Lebanese.
Identical in both MSA and Lebanese. One of the few prepositions that remains unchanged.
Very similar. Lebanese just drops the final short vowel: 'maʕ' instead of 'maʕa'.
Lebanese uses the simpler adjective form 'ʔarīb' (close). Note: Lebanese often pronounces 'q' as a glottal stop 'ʔ'.
MSA uses 'lā' for verbs and 'laysa' for nouns. Lebanese primarily uses 'ma' before verbs and 'mū' or 'mish' for nouns.
Lebanese wraps the verb in 'ma...sh' (circumfix negation), e.g., 'ma trūḥsh' (don't go). This is a defining feature of Lebanese grammar.
'Ma fī' literally means 'not there-is'. It is one of the most used expressions in Lebanese. MSA uses the passive verb 'yūjadu'.
Lebanese uses 'lissa' (still) + 'ma' (not) before the verb. MSA uses 'lam' (jussive particle) + 'baʕdu' (yet).
Lebanese 'mayy' comes from the same root but takes a different noun form. You will never hear 'māʔ' in everyday Lebanese speech.
Same root (kh-b-z). Lebanese shifts the first vowel and drops the tanwīn. The difference is mainly phonetic.
Lebanese pronounces 'q' as a glottal stop: 'ʔahwe' instead of 'qahwah'. This q-to-glottal shift is a key Lebanese sound change.
Nearly identical. Lebanese only drops the tanwīn ending. One of the closer MSA-Lebanese pairs.
Same root (l-ḥ-m). Lebanese drops tanwīn and adds the characteristic '-e' ending.
Lebanese drops the initial hamza and simplifies to 'rizz'. Shorter and more direct pronunciation.
Completely different words. Lebanese 'hallaʔ' (from 'hādhā al-waqt' = this time) replaces MSA 'al-ʔāna'. Very distinctive.
Different words. MSA 'ghadan' is never used in spoken Lebanese. 'Bukra' is universal across Levantine dialects.
Completely different. Lebanese 'mbēriḥ' has no clear MSA cognate. MSA 'ʔamsi' sounds literary to Lebanese ears.
Same word with minor phonetic changes: 'il-' instead of 'al-', dropped case ending, and slight vowel shift.
Lebanese uses 'baʕdēn' (after-that) which comes from 'baʕda ḥīn'. MSA 'lāḥiqan' is not used in speech.
Lebanese 'bass' replaces MSA 'lākinna/walākin'. 'Bass' also means 'enough/only', making it one of the most versatile Lebanese words.
Lebanese modifies 'liʔanna' to 'laʔanno' (with pronoun suffix). Also shortened to just 'la-' before some phrases.
Lebanese uses 'lamma' (from MSA 'lammā'). MSA 'ʕindamā' and 'ḥīnamā' are too formal for spoken Lebanese.
Same word but Lebanese pronounces 'dh' (ذ) as 'z'. This th/dh-to-z shift is systematic in Lebanese phonology.
Lebanese uses 'fa-' (same as MSA conjunction) or 'hēk' (like that/so). 'Lidhālika' is too formal for speech.
MSA 'thumma' is rarely heard in Lebanese. 'Baʕdēn' (afterwards) serves as the main sequential connector.
Lebanese uses the particle 'ʕam' (or 'ʕam-b-') before the verb to indicate ongoing action. MSA has no direct equivalent particle.
MSA uses 'sawfa' or 'sa-' prefix. Lebanese uses 'raḥ' (from 'rāyiḥ' = going to). Very similar to English 'going to'.
Lebanese adds the prefix 'b-' to indicate habitual action: 'byiḥki' = he (usually) speaks. This 'b-' prefix is a core Lebanese feature.
Both use 'law' for hypothetical conditionals. The verb form changes but the structure is similar. 'Law' is universal across Arabic varieties.
MSA has gendered relative pronouns (alladhī m., allatī f., etc.). Lebanese simplifies all to one word: 'yalli', used for all genders and numbers.
Lebanese uses the same 'yalli' where MSA would use 'man' for people. Context determines the meaning.
MSA 'mā' as a relative pronoun becomes 'yalli' or 'shū yalli' in Lebanese. 'Yalli' is the universal relative marker.
MSA uses Form VIII 'iʕtaqada'; Lebanese uses 'ftakar' with the habitual 'b-' prefix. 'ʔInno' replaces 'ʔanna'.
Very similar. Lebanese uses 'bi-' instead of 'fī' as the preposition, a common substitution throughout Lebanese.
Lebanese 'kaʔinno' (as-if) is used broadly where MSA uses 'yabdū' (it appears). Also heard as 'ka-ʔinno' or 'kinno'.
Lebanese uses the phrase 'maʕak ḥaʔʔ' (you have right/truth) rather than the MSA verb 'wāfaqa' (to agree).
Lebanese uses the elongated 'yīīī' or 'allah' for surprise. MSA's literary exclamation 'yā lal-ʕajab' is too formal for speech.
'Ya ḥarām' literally means 'oh, what a pity/sin'. It expresses sympathy or dismay and is deeply embedded in Lebanese culture.
Lebanese uses 'jadd?' (serious?) or 'bel-jadd?' (for-real?). Also common: 'maʕʔūl?' (is it reasonable?).
Same phrase but Lebanese contracts it into one fluid word: 'ʔinshalla'. Used constantly in daily speech for future plans or hope.
Nearly identical. Lebanese drops case endings and uses 'il-' instead of 'al-'. Used after meals, good news, or recovery from illness.
Lebanese commonly uses 'ʔustēz' (professor/teacher) as a respectful address for any man, even strangers. MSA 'sayyidī' is very formal.
Lebanese borrowed 'madām' from French. It is the standard polite address for women. MSA 'sayyidatī' sounds overly formal.
Lebanese uses 'baʕd ʔiznak' (with your permission) for polite interruption. 'ʕAfwan' is used more for 'you're welcome' in Lebanese.
Lebanese uses 'law samaḥt' (if you allow) or 'ʔiza btrīd' (if you want). 'Min faḍlak' also exists but sounds semi-formal.
Lebanese shortens to 'ʔakīd' (sure/certain). Also common: 'ṭabʕan' (naturally) which is shared with MSA.
Form II pattern is preserved in Lebanese but with simplified vowels. The doubled middle consonant remains the key feature.

items.concept: Form V (reflexive of II: to learn)
items.notes: Lebanese keeps the 't-' reflexive infix but shifts the prefix: 'yit-' instead of 'yata-'. The structure is recognizable.
Form VIII with its '-t-' infix is maintained in Lebanese. Vowel shifts follow the standard Lebanese pattern.
Form VII 'in-' prefix for passive/reflexive is preserved. 'nkisar' (it broke) is common in daily Lebanese speech.
Lebanese uses both the Arabic 'ijtimēʕ' and the English loanword 'mītīng'. Code-switching is common in Lebanese business.
Same root (sh-r-k). Lebanese shifts vowels and adds the characteristic '-e' ending instead of the tāʔ marbūṭa '-ah'.
Same word with the typical Lebanese simplification: dropped tanwīn and compressed initial vowel.
Different words. MSA 'rātib' exists in Lebanese but 'maʕāsh' (livelihood) is more commonly used for salary in daily speech.
Lebanese uses both the Arabic 'ʕaʔd' (with q-to-glottal shift) and the French loanword 'kōntra' (contrat).
Nearly identical. Lebanese drops the tanwīn. Directions are one area where MSA and Lebanese are quite close.
Lebanese uses 'shmēl' (from 'shimāl'). MSA 'yasār' is understood but not used in spoken Lebanese.
Lebanese 'dughri' is borrowed from Turkish ('dogru' = straight). It also means 'honest/direct' in a figurative sense.
Lebanese uses the simpler Form I 'laff' (to turn/wrap) instead of MSA's Form VIII 'iltafata'. Much shorter.
Lebanese uses 'ḥadd' (beside/limit) which is much more concise than MSA 'bi-jānib'. 'Ḥadd il-bēt' = next to the house.
Lebanese uses 'ḥakīm' (wise one, traditional) or the French-origin 'daktūr'. MSA 'ṭabīb' is understood but less common in speech.
Same word in both. One of the shared MSA/Lebanese medical terms. Only minor vowel shortening in Lebanese.
Same root (d-w-y). Lebanese drops the hamza and tanwīn: 'dawa' instead of 'dawāʔ'.
Lebanese prefers 'wajaʕ' (ache/pain) over MSA 'ʔalam'. 'Wajaʕ rēs' = headache, 'wajaʕ baṭn' = stomachache.
Same root (m-r-ḍ). Lebanese compresses the initial vowel and drops endings. Very similar to MSA.
MSA uses internal vowel change for passive (kutiba). Lebanese uses the Form VII 'in-' prefix (inkatab). The Lebanese method is more transparent.
MSA uses Form IV (ʔaf'ala). Lebanese prefers Form II doubling: 'ʔaʕʕad' (from ʔaʕad = to sit). The doubling makes the causative meaning.
Same pattern but Lebanese applies its standard phonetic changes: compressed vowels, '-e' ending. Verbal nouns are well-preserved across both varieties.
The active participle pattern (fā'il) is shared. In Lebanese, participles often function as present tense: 'kētib' can mean 'writing (right now)'.
Nearly identical. Lebanese drops tanwīn and slightly shifts the vowel. News vocabulary is heavily MSA-influenced even in dialects.
Lebanese uses either the Arabic 'maʔēle' (with q-to-glottal) or the English/French loanword 'ārtīkal'. Educated speakers may use either.
Lebanese uses 'maḥaṭṭa' (station) or the French 'shānīl' (chaîne). MSA 'qanāh' is understood but less natural in speech.
For 'live broadcast', both use similar terms. Lebanese often just says 'lāyv' (live) from English in casual speech.
Lebanese uses both the Arabic 'ṣaḥafi' and the French/English 'jūrnālīst'. Media vocabulary often mixes sources.
Same root (ḥ-r-r). Lebanese modifies the ending slightly. Abstract political vocabulary tends to be very close to MSA.
Same root (ʕ-d-l). Minor phonetic differences. Formal/abstract terms are often borrowed directly from MSA into Lebanese speech.
Very close in both varieties. Lebanese just simplifies the ending. 'Furṣa' is used identically in meaning.
Nearly identical. Abstract nouns ending in '-iyyah' are among the most stable words between MSA and Lebanese.
Lebanese uses 'ʔiza' (with z instead of dh) and often drops the 'fa-' result marker, relying on word order instead.
Structure is preserved. Only the pronunciation of 'ʔidhā' changes to 'ʔiza'. This is a case where Lebanese follows MSA structure.
Identical structure. 'Ḥatta law' works the same in both MSA and Lebanese. Very stable construction.
Lebanese breaks it into two words: 'ṭūl' (length/duration) + 'ma' (what/as). Same meaning, different form.
Same root (k-t-b). Lebanese applies standard simplification: 'yi-' prefix, dropped case endings, vowel reduction.
Lebanese drops the hamza and applies q-to-glottal shift: 'yiʔra' instead of 'yaqraʔu'. The root (q-r-ʔ) is the same.
Different verbs. MSA uses the formal Form V 'taḥaddatha'. Lebanese uses the simpler 'ḥaka' for everyday speaking.
Same word. Lebanese compresses the initial vowel cluster and drops tanwīn. Pronounced as one tight syllable: 'ktēb'.
Same root (q-ṣ-ṣ) with the characteristic Lebanese q-to-glottal shift. 'ʔIṣṣe' also informally means 'the issue/problem'.
Same root (ḥ-k-m). Political vocabulary is heavily MSA-influenced in Lebanese due to media exposure. Only minor ending changes.
Nearly identical. This is formal vocabulary that Lebanese speakers adopt almost directly from MSA.
Lebanese specifically says 'majlis in-nuwwēb' (council of deputies). The term is close to MSA 'majlis an-nuwwāb'.
Same word. Only the tanwīn is dropped. In Lebanon specifically refers to the President of the Republic.
Identical except for dropped tanwīn. Lebanon's confessional system makes political vocabulary especially common in daily discourse.
Lebanese uses both 'waṭan' (formal) and 'blēd' (country/homeland, from 'bilād'). 'Blēd' is more emotionally intimate.
Same root (h-j-r). Emigration vocabulary is deeply important in Lebanese culture given the massive diaspora worldwide.
Lebanese uses 'barra' (outside) for abroad. 'Rāḥ ʕa barra' (he went abroad) is a phrase heard in almost every Lebanese family.
Same root (j-l-w). 'Jēliye' specifically refers to the emigrant community. Lebanon has diaspora communities in Brazil, Australia, West Africa, and more.
Same word but Lebanese replaces 'th' (ث) with 's' sound, following the systematic th-to-s/t shift. 'Trēs' carries deep pride for Lebanese identity.
Contracted in Lebanese to one word. Used by all Lebanese regardless of religion. Can sincerely mean 'hopefully' or casually mean 'maybe/probably not'.
Used constantly across all religions in Lebanon. Said after eating, recovering from illness, or receiving good news. Nearly identical to MSA.
Same root (ṣ-l-w). Minor ending change. Lebanon's multi-confessional society means religious vocabulary is widely known but used with sensitivity.
Same root (b-r-k). 'Barake' is used in phrases like 'allah yibērik fīk' (God bless you), common across all Lebanese communities.
Lebanese restructures the sentence and uses a different verb: 'ḥamā' (to protect) instead of 'ḥafiẓa' (to preserve). The meaning is the same.
Same meaning but Lebanese restructures entirely. Uses 'byiḥki' (speaks) and 'ḥaki' (talk) reflecting the Lebanese verb 'ḥaka' instead of MSA 'qāla'.

items.concept: Patience is a virtue (Patience is the key to relief)
items.notes: This proverb is nearly identical in both varieties because it is widely known from religious and classical sources. Only minor phonetic shifts.

items.concept: The monkey in its mother's eye is a gazelle
items.notes: A beloved proverb meaning mothers see beauty in their children regardless. Lebanese version shows q-to-glottal, 'b-' for 'fī', and 'immo' for 'ʔummihi'.

items.concept: He who digs a pit for his brother falls in it

msa.arabic: مَنْ حَفَرَ حُفْرَةً لِأَخِيهِ وَقَعَ فِيهَا
msa.transliteration: man ḥafara ḥufratan li-ʔakhīhi waqaʕa fīhā

items.notes: Lebanese replaces 'man' with 'yalli', uses the 'b-' habitual prefix, and says 'ghēro' (other/someone else) instead of 'ʔakhīhi' (his brother).
Same root (q-l-b) with the Lebanese q-to-glottal shift. 'ʔAlbi' (my heart) is an extremely common term of endearment in Lebanese.
Nearly identical. 'Rūḥi' (my soul) is used as a term of endearment, like 'darling'. 'Rūḥ' also means 'go!' as an imperative in Lebanese.
Same root (sh-w-q) with the Lebanese q-to-glottal shift. 'Shōʔ' appears in poetry and love songs. 'Ishtaʔtelak' = I missed/longed for you.
Different words. Lebanese prefers 'ḥilw' (sweet/beautiful) over MSA 'jamīl'. 'Jamīl' is understood but sounds literary. 'Ḥilw/ḥilwe' is used for people, food, and situations.
Same word with minimal changes. 'Fajir' retains its poetic weight in Lebanese. Fairuz famously sings about dawn and Lebanon's mountains.
Same root (n-j-m). Lebanese compresses the initial syllable. Stars feature prominently in Lebanese folk poetry and Zajal traditions.
